DISCOVERY 302, NO APPEARANCES. THE COURT ADOPTS THE TENTATIVE RULING: PLAINTIFF SANAA HAMS' MOTION TO COMPEL DISCOVERY RESPONSES, TO PRODUCE DOCUMENTS, TO COMPEL DEPOSITION, AND FOR SANCTIONS IS CONTINUED TO JANUARY 24, 2024 AT 9:00 AM IN DEPARTMENT 301 BEFORE JPT MCDONALD. THE COURT NOTES THAT THE PRESENT MOTION WAS FILED DURING A CHANGE IN COUNSEL FOR DEFENDANTS. THE COURT BELIEVES THAT FURTHER MEET AND CONFER DISCUSSIONS ARE REQUIRED ON THE WRITTEN DISCOVERY. THE PARTIES SHALL MEET AND CONFER VIA ZOOM BY DECEMBER 22, 2023. PLAINTIFF IS DIRECTED TO FILE A SUPPLEMENTAL SEPARATE STATEMENT OF ITEMS IN DISPUTE BY JANUARY 9, 2024. DEFENDANTS ARE DIRECTED TO FILE ANY OPPOSING SEPARATE STATEMENT BY JANUARY 18, 2024. PARTIES SHALL ALSO EMAIL SUBSEQUENT FILINGS TO JPT MCDONALD. AS FOR THE DEPOSITIONS, THE PARTIES SHALL MEET AND CONFER AND ESTABLISH CONFIRMED DATES FOR THE DEPOSITIONS BASED ON THE AVAILABILITY OF THE WITNESSES AND COUNSEL. THE COURT WILL HOLD A HEARING ON JANUARY 24, 2024 TO EVALUATE WHETHER ITS ORDERS HAVE BEEN COMPLIED WITH AND WHETHER FURTHER ORDERS ARE NECESSARY. THE PREVAILING PARTY IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE A PROPOSED ORDER REPEATING VERBATIM THE SUBSTANTIVE PORTION OF THE COURT'S RULING IN COMPLIANCE WITH CRC 3.1312 (APPROVAL AS TO FORM) AND MUST E-MAIL IT TO THE JUDGE PRO TEM AT DAVIDIDAHO14@GMAIL.COM. DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-18-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. INNA BRADY, Esq. of IB Law Firm (tel: 415-237-3938) Defendants.
No other appearances made in this matter.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT ALESSANDRO RAIMONDI, PARTENOPEA COMPANY, LLC's DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

Matter not argued/contested. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Alessandro Raimondi and Partenopea Company, LLC's (defendants') demurrers to the third amended complaint (TAC) are sustained in part and overruled in part. The case involves disputes over an Italian restaurant in the Castro.
Defendants first demur to TAC counts 1-10 and 14 (employment claims), "because Plaintiff is not an employee of the Defendants." (Notice 3:5-6.) However, the TAC pleads that the defendants treated plaintiff as an employee - e.g., by issuing wage statements to her that listed the restaurant as the employer and plaintiff as an employee. (Id. at 8:26-9:6; see also Kao v. Holiday (2017) 12 Cal.App.5th 947, 960-62.) This suffices for the pleading stage; the demurrer to TAC counts 1-10 and 14 is overruled.
Defendants second demur to TAC counts 11 and 12 (intentional and negligent misrepresentation), "because the alleged statements are not past or existing facts and Plaintiff failed to satisfy the heightened standard of pleading." (Notice 3:10-12.) Plaintiff's opposition does not address the second point (Opp. 9:17-11:10) and her pleadings fail as to it. (Stansfield v. Starkey (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 59, 73 ["how, when, where, to whom, and by what means the representations were tendered"].) The demurrer is thus sustained; plaintiff is given a fifth and final chance to plead cognizable misrepresentation claims against defendants.
Defendants third demur to TAC count 15 (breach of contract), "because Plaintiff failed to allege damages." (Notice 3:13-14.) Damages are alleged (see, e.g., TAC 10:11-13, 10:19-20, 23:21-24:16), so this demurrer is overruled.
Defendants fourth demur to TAC count 16 ("breach of good faith"), "because Plaintiff failed to allege deprivation from the benefits of the Agreement." (Notice 3:15-16.) Deprivations are alleged (see, e.g., TAC 10:11-11:8, 24:17-25:20), so this demurrer is overruled.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RBU)

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-18-2024 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. INNA BRADY, Esq. of IB Law Firm (tel: 415-237-3938) Defendants.
No other appearances made in this matter.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT DANEIL STELTER's DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

Matter not argued/contested. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Daniel Stelter's demurrers to the third amended complaint (TAC) are sustained. The case involves disputes over an Italian restaurant in the Castro.
Stetler first demurs to TAC counts 1-10 and 14 (employment claims), because no employment relationship ever existed between plaintiff and Stetler - he "never hired her, was never an owner of the business, and was never a party to the operating agreement of" the business. (Notice 1:11-14.) The latter two points are established by the operating agreements attached to (and thus made part of) the TAC (Exhs. A-D), and the TAC nowhere contends that Stetler himself hired plaintiff. Plaintiff relies on an alter ego theory. (Opp. 8:11-26.) However, alter ego applies to make a corporation's owners liable for its acts (Sonora Diamond Corp. v. Sup. Ct. (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 523, 538) and the TAC establishes that Stetler was never an owner. In any event, a "determination that a person is the alter ego of a corporation does not make the alter ego an employer." (Leek v. Cooper (2011) 194 Cal.App.4th 399, 409.) The TAC is plaintiff's fourth attempt to plead cognizable claims, indicating that employment law claims cannot lie against Stetler. His demurrer to TAC counts 1-10 and 14 is thus sustained without leave to amend.
Stetler second demurs to TAC counts 11 and 12 (intentional and negligent misrepresentation) "because the alleged statements are not past or existing facts and Plaintiff failed to satisfy the heightened standard of pleading." (Notice 3:6-8.) Plaintiff's opposition fails to address the second point (Opp. 10:9-11:20) and her pleadings fail as to it. (Stansfield v. Starkey (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 59, 73 ["how, when, where, to whom, and by what means the representations were tendered"].) The demurrer is thus sustained; plaintiff is given a fifth and final chance to plead cognizable misrepresentation claims against Stetler.
Finally, Stetler demurs to TAC counts 15 and 16 (breach of contract and "breach of good faith"). Plaintiff again relies on her unavailing alter ego theory. (Opp. 11:22-23.) These demurrers are sustained without leave to amend.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RBU)

Notice Of Motion For An Order: 1) Appointing Receiver; And 2) Issuing Preliminary Injunction In Aid Of Receiver (Motions)
Off Calendar Oct-18-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF SANAA HAMS' Motion For An Order: 1) Appointing Receiver; And 2) Issuing Preliminary Injunction in Aid of Receiver.

Plaintiff's "motion for an order: 1) appointing receiver; and 2) issuing preliminary injunction in aid of receiver" in this dispute over a restaurant is denied for several reasons.

First, as plaintiff concedes, receivership is an "extraordinary remedy" (Memo. 9:7); it is "drastic" and "exercised sparingly" and unwarranted here. (In re Jamison Steel Corp. (1958) 158 Cal.App.2d 27, 35; Morand v. Sup. Ct. (1974) 38 Cal.App.3d 347, 350.)

Second, plaintiff fails to carry her burden to show that the property "is in danger of being lost, removed, or materially injured." (CCP 564(b)(1).) Her statement of facts is largely unsupported by citation and key points are on "information" and/or "belief." (Memo. 2:3-8:8; Hams Dec. 8:9, 8:14, 8:22, 9:4.)

Third, in an industry notorious for low margins, receiver costs could cripple the restaurant. (Opp. 14:1-28; City and County of San Francisco v. Daley (1993) 16 Cal.App.4th 734, 744.)
Fourth, this action was filed sixth months ago; the delay in seeking a receiver is unexplained and further shows that the "drastic remedy" is unwarranted. (See Jamison, 158 Cal.App.2d at 35.)

Order is signed.
